1db61e77-786d-4d4c-94e0-4dcc221af20b.product_description:
We’re seeing this a lot in the automation space, and honestly, I’ve been guilty of it too.

Most automation agencies make one mistake, they try to sell automation.
But selling automation often adds cost before it adds value.

Because in the early stages, most of what automation promises can actually be done manually, faster, cheaper, and with more context.

The real need for automation begins when manual work becomes the bottleneck, when scaling becomes painful, repetitive, and error-prone.

That’s when automation stops being a “nice-to-have” and becomes an enabler of growth.

So instead of trying to sell automation, we build it where the pain is real, where teams are already doing the work, and scaling it manually no longer makes sense.

At CodingRippler, that’s how we think about automation,
not as a product to push, but as a capability that emerges naturally when scaling requires it.

Maybe automation shouldn’t be sold, it should be discovered.

 Curious if your team has reached the point where automation actually makes sense?
Let’s explore that together → [

 Want to see how other teams identified the right moments to automate and what impact it created?
Explore our case studies → [

1db61e77-786d-4d4c-94e0-4dcc221af20b.product_description:
Meet our new CreativeAgent from ImagineCreate AI — an AI agent you can talk to that creates images, videos, and more. Think of it as a full creative agency at your fingertips!

For the demo, I gave it a single image of a jacket and asked it to put the jacket on 3 models with different ethnicities, walking in Paris, New York, and London. Check out the results 

What CreativeAgent can do:
 Swap garments & backgrounds
 Change camera angles
 Generate variations across models & scenes
 Bulk process assets
 Edit results instantly

Brands can now create ad assets, social content, and homepage visuals faster than ever. Available on Starter, Advanced, and Pro plans starting today. 

#AI #Creativity #Innovation #MCP #Agents #AgenticCommerce #FashionTech #ImageGeneration

prediction.review_text:
I'm thrilled to introduce CreativeAgent from ImagineCreate AI! As the founder of CodingRippler, I’m always on the lookout for innovative tools that can enhance our automation and AI workflows. Recently, I challenged CreativeAgent to create variations of a jacket on models of different ethnicities in iconic cities like Paris, New York, and London. The results were not just impressive; they highlighted the importance of diversity in representation, which is crucial in today’s creative landscape.

What excites me most is how this tool empowers brands to generate engaging ad assets and social content with remarkable efficiency. The ability to swap garments, change backgrounds, and edit results instantly opens up new avenues for creativity and collaboration.

In our tech and creative fields, we have a pivotal opportunity to leverage AI for more inclusive storytelling. Let’s embrace this innovation and explore how it can elevate our work while fostering trust and transparency in our teams. I’d love to hear your thoughts on how we can utilize such tools to recognize diverse work styles and enhance collaboration!

#AI #Creativity #Innovation #DiversityInTech #AgenticCommerce #Collaboration

1db61e77-786d-4d4c-94e0-4dcc221af20b.product_description:
85% of AI projects fail to deliver value. 

Air Canada learned this expensively when their chatbot gave incorrect information, leading to tribunal rulings and legal liability. 

Most companies are evaluating LLMs like databases when they should be evaluating them like adaptive biological systems. 

As someone who's built neural control systems for stroke recovery and cancer patient triage, I see the same evaluation mistakes happening across industries. 

I just published a deep dive on why traditional "accuracy testing" fails for LLMs and how to evaluate them properly [linked below]. 

Are you deploying LLMs in your organization? The evaluation framework you choose today determines whether you'll join the 85% that fail or the 15% that succeed. 

﻿#AIStrategy #EnterpriseAI #RiskManagement

2e4a00af-909d-4ee2-9aa6-52eee25d18e9.product_description:
Celebrating 5 Years at Microsoft 

If you had told me when I graduated from WVU in 2014 with a Bachelor of Arts in Criminology that I would one day be a Senior Software Engineer at Microsoft with a Master of Science in Software Engineering, I might not have believed you. Yet here I am in 2025, reflecting on an incredible journey filled with growth, opportunity, and gratitude.

First, I owe so much to GEICO’s Veterans IT Development Program, which gave me my start in programming and changed the trajectory of my career. A heartfelt thanks also to Deloitte, where I learned invaluable soft skills and the true power of an inclusive and collaborative organization.

I’m deeply grateful to the West Virginia Army National Guard for enabling me to earn my master’s degree debt-free through their exceptional tuition assistance programs. If you’re looking for the best deal in education, this is it!

My time as a commissioned Field Artillery Officer in the Army National Guard has taught me leadership and resilience in ways that no classroom or corporate training ever could.

To all the incredible civilian and military leaders and mentors I’ve had the privilege of learning from over the years—your guidance and support have truly shaped who I am today. Your impact on my life has been profound, and I am forever grateful.

This journey is a testament to the power of hard work and having clear goals. It truly is the American dream.

I’m incredibly thankful, humbled, and blessed to be where I am today. Here’s to the next chapter!

#Microsoft #SoftwareEngineering #ArmyNationalGuard #Gratitude #AmericanDream

prediction.review_text:
Today marks a significant milestone—5 years at Microsoft! 🎉 Reflecting on my journey since graduating from WVU in 2014 with a Bachelor of Arts in Criminology, I am filled with immense gratitude for the experiences and mentors who have shaped my career. 

I owe a huge debt of thanks to GEICO’s Veterans IT Development Program, which ignited my passion for programming and set me on this path. My time at Deloitte was equally transformative, where I honed invaluable soft skills and learned the true power of collaboration in an inclusive environment. 

I’m especially grateful to the West Virginia Army National Guard for their exceptional tuition assistance programs, allowing me to earn my master’s degree debt-free. The leadership and resilience I developed as a commissioned Field Artillery Officer have been instrumental in my professional growth. 

To all the incredible mentors and leaders—both civilian and military—who have guided me along the way, your support has profoundly impacted my life. I am forever thankful for your wisdom and encouragement. 

This journey is a testament to hard work, clear goals, and the power of mentorship. Here’s to the next chapter, and to all of you navigating your own paths—never underestimate the impact of a mentor! Let's continue to uplift and support one another in our journeys. 

#Microsoft #SoftwareEngineering #VeteransInTech #Mentorship #Gratitude

2e4a00af-909d-4ee2-9aa6-52eee25d18e9.product_description:
Change is Coming: The Army Fitness Test Evolves

Starting June 1, 2025, the Army is implementing the new Army Fitness Test (AFT) - a five-event assessment built to meet the demands of modern warfighting.

This isn’t just about removing the standing power throw or renaming the ACFT. It’s about raising the bar for readiness, lethality, and shared standards.

Key changes:
• AFT becomes the official test of record
• 5 events: Deadlift, Push-Up, Sprint-Drag-Carry, Plank, 2-Mile Run
• Sex-neutral, age-normed standards for 21 combat MOSs
• Min score: 350 total / 60 per event (combat MOSs)
• Min score: 300 total (combat-support MOSs)

This change reinforces our commitment to excellence - regardless of rank, MOS, or gender.

We lead with standards.

Are you ready to meet the challenge?

#ArmyFitnessTest #WarfighterStandards #AJOC #ArmyStrong #JuniorOfficers #CombatReady #WarriorEthos #Lethality #Readiness 

The Army Leader Exchange The Center for Junior Officers Kris Saling US Army

prediction.review_text:
As we prepare for the rollout of the new Army Fitness Test (AFT) on June 1, 2025, I can't help but reflect on the vital role adaptability and resilience play in our military standards. This five-event assessment is more than just a test; it embodies our commitment to excellence and readiness across all ranks and MOSs. 

The introduction of sex-neutral, age-normed standards is a significant step towards inclusivity, ensuring that every service member can rise to the challenges of modern warfare. 

In my role as a Senior Software Engineer at Microsoft, I see parallels in how we tackle challenges in technology. Just as we elevate military fitness standards, we must also enhance our benchmarks in cloud security and compliance. It’s about fostering teamwork, clear communication, and a shared vision for success. 

Are you ready to embrace this evolution? Let’s lead by example and ensure we are all combat-ready! 💪 #ArmyFitnessTest #WarfighterStandards #ArmyStrong #Teamwork #Readiness

3a0a7529-16a1-48d1-b987-c60e4260ad46.product_description:
This week marks my last at solo.io - it's been a great two years, I've learned a *ton*, enjoyed working with some very smart people, accepted a maintainership for a relatively mature and complex open-source project (Istio), gotten to do lots of interesting low-level Linux stuff, developed a very strong appreciation for both Rust/Tokio, and new respect for *anyone* that takes on the challenge of writing spec-complete ` implementations :P

Extreme shoutout to Lin Sun who gave me this (and many other) opportunities and has been an incredible mentor and boss - Lin is a massive asset to the open-source community as a whole, and someone I genuinely respect and trust on a personal level as well.

solo.io is in a remarkably strong position in the market now - the most powerful OSS K8S gateway offering (kgateway) + the fastest, cheapest and most invisible way to secure cluster traffic with mTLS (Istio Ambient) - and a ton of great people.

It's been great working with you all, I'm off to Edera to help plug the well-known gaps in K8S workload isolation!

prediction.review_text:
As I wrap up my journey at solo.io, I want to take a moment to express my heartfelt gratitude for the incredible experiences I've had over the past two years. Working on complex open-source projects like Istio has been both a challenge and a privilege. A massive shoutout to Lin Sun, whose mentorship has been invaluable; your guidance has significantly shaped my growth, and I truly appreciate all the opportunities you've provided. 🌟

Solo.io is in a fantastic position with its innovative K8S gateway and mTLS solutions, and I’m excited to see where the future takes them. Collaborating with such talented individuals has been a highlight of my career.

Now, I’m thrilled to announce my next chapter at Edera, where I’ll be focusing on bridging the gaps in K8S workload isolation. Let’s continue to push the boundaries of what’s possible in cloud-native tech together! 💪 #Kubernetes #OpenSource #CloudNative

Some Thoughts on Development

As a developer, I care a lot about the scalability of the systems I build and like to think long-term.

But that’s not always the case. Sometimes, I realize I just need to kick it off and get it done. There are times when I don’t need to over-engineer the code or create long-term solutions. Real-world problems can be quite different from ideal system designs.

For instance, if we’re building a proof of concept, it might be more important to deliver quickly than to ensure the system can handle millions of users. We can always refine and scale it later if the concept proves successful.

prediction.review_text:
As a software engineer at Microsoft, I often find myself navigating the balance between ideal system design and the practical realities we face in development. 🚀 

I've come to realize that sometimes, especially when working on proof of concepts, speed can take precedence over perfection. It's crucial to prioritize delivering a working solution rather than getting caught up in over-engineering. Real-world challenges can be unpredictable, and agility often leads to the best outcomes.

Reflecting on my experiences, I've learned that maintaining context in our coding practices is vital. It’s not just about the code we write, but how we adapt and evolve it to meet the needs of our users. Let's embrace the learning process and support each other in this journey! 💡

#SoftwareDevelopment #Agile #RealWorldCoding #Adaptability

Attention all friends and fellow engineers! I'm reaching out to you today because I'm in dire need of your help. Unfortunately, some truly exceptional engineers were let go from our team this week. These colleagues have been instrumental in my growth and development within the company, and I've learned so much from them

That's why I'm reaching out to you, my trusted network. I was hoping that perhaps someone out there might be able to offer a referral for these amazing engineers. They are not only incredibly talented, but also wonderful individuals to work with. They have a passion for their craft that is unmatched, and their expertise has made a significant impact on our team.

So, if you or someone you know is looking to hire talented and driven engineers, please don't hesitate to reach out. I would be forever grateful for your help in finding a new home for these amazing individuals. Together, we can help them land on their feet and continue to thrive in their careers. Thank you in advance for your help!

Some additional info:
- These engineers are based in Vancouver or in the US
- Focused more on backend development though have full stack experience

Edit: Thanks for all the help everyone! Please keep them coming!

583f07ae-71cc-42ed-982b-67b6c6d6a3ff.product_description:
A question for my network — genuinely curious about your experience.

First, let me be clear where I stand: I'm a believer in the full testing pyramid. Unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, contract testing, property-based testing, mutation testing, performance testing, resilience testing, chaos engineering — all of it. Each layer catches different things, and I've seen the value at every level.

My question is narrower. It's about what we mean by "unit" in unit testing.

To be fair, I see value in the class-level, mock-heavy approach — especially for coordination layers where you genuinely care that the right services get called in the right order. It's also useful as a design feedback tool: painful mock setup often signals architectural problems. I'm not proposing we abandon it. I'm proposing we layer it with behavior-level testing. Ideally, I'd want both — full coverage at every layer of the pyramid, including both styles of unit testing. The only tests I'd skip are the truly redundant ones (I once saw a team unit test a pure delegation layer that was already exercised by every other layer — that's just noise).

Saying this, I'm not sure the way most teams do unit testing actually helps when it matters most (as a non-regression safety net during refactoring/upgrades that is there to support & encourage such ongoing refactoring).

Here's what I mean. The moment I need tests the most is during refactoring — when I'm restructuring code but want behavior to stay the same. Tests should be my safety net.

But if my tests are written at the class level, mocking every collaborator, then refactoring *breaks the tests*. Not because behavior changed, but because the internal wiring changed. Now I'm rewriting tests and code at the same time, and I've lost my independent witness. I'm not refactoring anymore — I'm rewriting with hope.

The tests became a cage, not a net.

This got me digging into history. When Kent Beck wrote about TDD, a "unit" meant a unit of *behavior* — something meaningful from the perspective of what the system does. Somewhere along the way, particularly as the London school of TDD grew influential, "unit" quietly became "class" or even "method." The vocabulary shifted, and perhaps the intent got lost.

What I've started exploring: testing at the level of functional behavior — a cluster of classes that together deliver a capability. Still unit testing (not integration testing — no real databases, no network calls). Mock at architectural boundaries, not at internal collaboration points. This way, when I refactor the internals, the tests still protect me.

What surprises me is that I hear test architects and senior QA folks talk about this openly, but among developers it's less common in my experience.

Have others navigated this tension? What patterns have worked for your teams — especially at scale? And for those who've studied TDD history more deeply: am I reading this evolution correctly?

Genuinely curious to learn.

prediction.review_text:
I've been reflecting on the concept of 'unit' in unit testing, especially as we navigate the complexities of refactoring. It's fascinating how the definition has shifted from a focus on meaningful behavior to often being tied to classes or methods. 🤔 

I believe we should be testing at the level of functional behavior—grouping classes that deliver capabilities together. This approach not only protects us during refactoring but also keeps our tests relevant and effective. 

I'm genuinely curious about your experiences with this. What patterns have worked for your teams, especially at scale? Let's share insights and help each other refine our testing strategies! #UnitTesting #TDD #SoftwareDevelopment

actual.review_text:
My experience has been that the role of unit tests is to both model the real-world scenarios you'd encounter in production and to test the framework. If a change to the framework occurs you need to change the tests too, those should be fairly straightforward and simple, and safe to regenerate with AI. They provide the least value, guarding only against minor changes that don't alter function signatures or ifaces, they're there to help you when someone changed a label, added an if to some internals, etc.

Situations where "unit" is what should be used as placeholder the meaning of functionality, which may sometimes require some mocking of other subsystems external to your code in order to keep focus on the outcome you're trying to model, are far more valuable and require good separation of concerns.
My exp has been that the minimal API-mocking ones are easy to implement and usually get the time they require, while the latter (which ironically provide far better value) get deferred and may get attention when someone that spent 2 months on some complicated investigation decides to invest that time without approval. I don't really care what people call unit, but I care that the functional tests exist. And a lot of times they don't.

I'm a firm believer in the full testing pyramid—unit tests, integration tests, and all the layers in between. Each layer plays a crucial role in catching different issues. However, I think we need to rethink what we mean by 'unit' testing. 

What if we focused on testing at the level of functional behavior? Grouping classes that work together to deliver a capability could provide a more robust safety net during refactoring. When I refactor, I want my tests to protect me, not break because of internal wiring changes. 

I’ve noticed that while this conversation is common among test architects and QA professionals, it seems less prevalent among developers. Have any of you navigated this tension? What patterns have worked for your teams, especially at scale? I’m genuinely curious to learn from your experiences! #UnitTesting #TDD #SoftwareDevelopment

583f07ae-71cc-42ed-982b-67b6c6d6a3ff.product_description:
Office culture is dying 
 
A groundbreaking study recently revealed a staggering 65% of employees asserting that their productivity would skyrocket when working from the comforts of their own home. 
 
Think about it - no time wasted on travel, more freedom to work as you like, and a perfect blend of work and personal life. And with today's tech, your team can work together no matter where they are! 
 
Still not sure about switching to remote work? Think again! 
 
The future is here, and it's remote!

prediction.review_text:
As remote work continues to reshape our professional landscape, I find it fascinating that a recent study shows 65% of employees believe their productivity would significantly increase when working from home. 🚀 

Having transitioned to remote work myself, I can personally vouch for the benefits: no commuting, the flexibility to structure my day, and a healthier work-life balance. However, it’s crucial to recognize that this shift also highlights the importance of diverse work styles. 

Every team member has unique preferences and strengths that contribute to our collective success. Embracing these differences is key to fostering an inclusive and productive remote environment. Let’s leverage technology not just to connect, but to truly collaborate and innovate, regardless of where we are. 

The future is indeed remote, and I’m excited to see how we can adapt and grow together! 💻 #RemoteWork #DiverseWorkStyles #TeamDynamics

prediction.review_text:
As I reflect on the evolving dynamics of remote work, I can't help but think about the recent study that revealed 65% of employees believe their productivity would significantly increase while working from home. This resonates deeply with my own experiences—eliminating the commute and having the flexibility to create my ideal workspace has truly transformed my output. 

However, we must also recognize the complexities that accompany this shift. While the freedom to work remotely is a tremendous advantage, it can lead to communication gaps and feelings of isolation that impact team dynamics. It's crucial for us to address these challenges head-on to ensure our collective success. 

Fostering a culture of transparency and inclusivity in our remote setups is essential. Every team member should feel connected and valued, regardless of their location. Let's embrace this shift not just for productivity's sake, but to cultivate a supportive and engaging workplace culture that thrives on collaboration. 

How are you navigating these changes within your teams? I’d love to hear your insights and experiences! #RemoteWork #TeamCulture #FutureOfWork

Should you be open with colleagues about what you earn? In many industries it is still taboo — and in some companies, so-called ‘pay secrecy’ is openly encouraged. 

Discussing money has also long been held as an impolite topic of conversation, but sharing your salary with others can have positive effects. It can help to close wage gaps that exist between genders and minorities at the hiring step, but also when negotiating pay rises. Source: 

What do you think about being ‘salary transparent’? Vote in our poll and tell us why in the comments.

actual.review_text: Transparency should be implemented at company level and it should be presented as brackets, not individual salaries. Based on personal merit and impact on company any individual progresses towards the higher part of the bracket until promoted. The first place to start with this transparency should be in job ads though, many times the lack of this transparency encourages personal statistics made by asking friends who don't really do the job you're applying for or searching inaccurate websites causing many people to make bad decisions of either asking too much or too little in interviews. Transparency can also be a motivating factor, to know what one could earn more by doing some specific type of development can make some people have clear career paths early on, provided they have the dedication, ability and if it's also interesting to them.

7333df73-63cf-4f92-b724-caefc9171b63.product_description:
I've successfully completed the learning path "Become a C++ Developer" from LinkedIn Learning, a learning path which consists of over 17 hours of C++ theory and in-depth exercises!

My favorite module of the learning path was and still is "Web Servers and APIs Using C++", by Troy Miles.

The module was outstanding, I've learnt how to create and automate Web Servers with C++, deploy resources using Docker, include HTML5, CSS3 and PHP elements and, last but not least, integrate external resources such as MongoDB to the Web Servers.

I'm very grateful for everything that I've learnt and this is just the start!

I recommend the learning path "Become a C++ Developer" for every C++ passionate programmer and for everyone else who would like to enrichen their C++ knowledge.

Link to the LinkedIn Learning Path: 

Thank you, everyone!

#developer #docker #programming #cplusplus #objectorientedprogramming #linkedinlearning

937353b2-546c-41e3-ae57-4b7507de8a1d.product_description:
Why Mahjong is the New LinkedIn for women in Tech

No booths.
No name tags.
Just tiles, stories, laughter, and some serious business ideas.

Yes, Mahjong.
The game you may remember made famous from the movie Crazy rich Asians ?

It’s making a massive comeback, especially among women in tech and AI.
Why?
Because we are looking for more than cold intros and badge scans.
We want real conversations.
We want to connect, collaborate, and build, together.

Here’s what’s happening right now:
Mahjong events are up 180% across the U.S. (Eventbrite data)
San Francisco, LA, and Austin are leading the movement
Founders are replacing VC mixers with Mahjong Nights
Startups are hosting warehouse tournaments with disco balls (!)

And the best part?
There’s no pitch deck required.
Just curiosity, strategy, and room to be yourself.
It’s being called the “IRL LinkedIn for women.”
And honestly, it works.

As someone who speaks on AI, builds in the Bay Area, and shows up for Women in Technology (WIT) and Women in AI communities…
This feels like the future of networking:
Warm, playful, smart, and deeply human.

So if you're building something big, or just looking to belong, Don’t overlook the mahjong table.

The next investor, co-founder, or collaborator might be sitting right across from you.

Let’s rewrite the rules of networking, one tile at a time.
Who’s down for a game?

937353b2-546c-41e3-ae57-4b7507de8a1d.product_description:
Yesterday marked the final day of my Explore (SWE + TPM) Internship at Xbox! 

This summer was an incredible journey of learning and growth. I joined the Experiences and Platforms for Developers: Creator Onboarding, Resources, and Support (XPD-CORS) team, contributing to a greenfield project aimed at transforming how Xbox supports creators in play testing their games.

As a TPM, I led partner studio discussions, authored a feature spec, and collaborated across time zones with a designer to produce Figma designs.

As a SWE, I designed, built, and deployed the backend service, API, and data layer for an end-to-end proof of concept, successfully demoing it to XPD multiple times.

Other highlights included placing 3rd in the Intern Celebration Hackathon, earning 2nd in Halo Reach: Firefight during the Xbox Intern Gaming Tournament, winning a tour of Mojang Studios!

I also played a ton of Sand Volleyball and started reading One Piece

I’m deeply grateful to those who made this internship unforgettable:
 My pod, the ThunderDome — Sunday Ochigbo and Raul Ballines (FIFA Fridays forever, trust).
 My manager Suvir Arora and (formerly) Alex Blount 
 My mentors Brian Trevethan and Greg Gonyea 
 The team — Anthony Keller, Maria Lopez Quesada, Daniel Jones, David Kushmerick, Kyle Sweeney, Jason Williams, Eddie Iglesias III, George Ju, and Ai Fukuda 
 Jerry Zhu for being an invaluable mentor to the ThunderDome and the best step-podmate
 My interviewers and recruiter Luis Naranjo, Karla Larriva, and Jessica Nguyen for helping make this experience possible.
 My hackathon team Greenlens: Jada Sheppard, Geo Johnson, Sloan Howard, Yemi Afolayanka, Temiloluwa Okelowo, and Tryphena Bossman 

A huge thank you as well to all the Xbox interns and to Intern Program lead Teresa Wong for the incredible events and building such an amazing community. 

All the challenges, guidance, encouragement, and camaraderie made my first internship a truly unforgettable one. 

#Microsoft #Xbox #Internship #GameDevelopment #Tech #MicrosoftExplore #MicrosoftIntern #XboxIntern

Yesterday marked the final day of my Explore (SWE + TPM) Internship at Xbox! 🎉 Reflecting on this summer, I am overwhelmed with gratitude for the incredible journey of learning and growth. Joining the XPD-CORS team was a unique opportunity to contribute to a greenfield project aimed at transforming how Xbox supports creators in play testing their games.

As a TPM, I had the privilege of leading discussions with partner studios and authoring a feature spec, all while collaborating across time zones with a talented designer to create impactful Figma designs. On the SWE side, I designed, built, and deployed the backend service, API, and data layer for an end-to-end proof of concept, successfully demoing it multiple times to the XPD team.

I want to take a moment to express my heartfelt gratitude to everyone who made this journey unforgettable. Your guidance, support, and camaraderie were invaluable. 

Special thanks to my pod, the ThunderDome — Sunday Ochigbo and Raul Ballines for the laughs and FIFA Fridays. To my manager Suvir Arora and (formerly) Alex Blount for your leadership. To my mentors Brian Trevethan and Greg Gonyea for your invaluable insights. And to my amazing team: Anthony Keller, Maria Lopez Quesada, David Kushmerick, Kyle Sweeney, Jason Williams, Eddie Iglesias III, George Ju, and Ai Fukuda. A special shoutout to Jerry Zhu for being an invaluable mentor and step-podmate.

I also want to thank my interviewers and recruiter Luis Naranjo, Karla Larriva, and Jessica Nguyen for making this experience possible. And of course, my hackathon team Greenlens: Jada Sheppard, Geo Johnson, Sloan Howard, Yemi Afolayanka, Temiloluwa Okelowo, and Tryphena Bossman.

A huge thank you to all the Xbox interns and to Intern Program lead Teresa Wong for creating such an incredible community. The challenges, guidance, and friendships made my first internship truly unforgettable. Excited for what’s next! 

#Microsoft #Xbox #Internship #GameDevelopment #MicrosoftExplore

ab1fb966-d14e-4639-b504-00f7b6f9084b.product_description:
Encerro um ciclo de 13 anos na Microsoft, uma jornada que me trouxe aprendizados imensos, grandes realizações e a oportunidade de viver o sonho de trabalhar em uma das maiores empresas de tecnologia do mundo. Tive experiências que ultrapassaram fronteiras, colaborando com clientes e colegas em projetos no Brasil e no exterior. Foi um privilégio participar na linha de frente de momentos marcantes e escrever uma parte da história da Microsoft, como a liderança em Inteligência Artificial, o fortalecimento e expansão do Azure e a evolução da engenharia de software através do Visual Studio, Azure DevOps e posteriormente com GitHub, impactando centenas de milhares de desenvolvedores ao longo desses anos nos quais tive oportunidade de visitar, conhecer e conversar.

Nada disso teria sido possível sem as pessoas incríveis que estiveram ao meu lado nessa trajetória. Sou profundamente grato a todos que me apoiaram, inspiraram e me ensinaram em cada projeto, conversa e desafio. Além de colegas, fiz amigos para a vida toda e sou grato por cada história vivida juntos.

Chegou o momento de iniciar uma nova jornada, ela será interessante e diferente do que já trabalhei, uma boa mudança que trará experiência complementar de tudo que vivi nesses 13 anos de Microsoft, mas essas novidades deixarei para uma próxima história (post)!

Christiano Faig Alessandro Loesch Jannuzzi Stéfany Mazon Carlos Abramo Paula Silva Gustavo Zimmermann Lalo (Eduardo) Steinmann Carlos Zimmermann Fernando Filiputti Iury Oliveira Livia Regina Bijelli Ferreira João Paulo Fernandes Vinícius Souza Marcel Aldecoa Allan Targino Fernando de Oliveira Fabrício Catae Luiz Ávila Junior Daniel Consolim Oliveira João Carlos Bolonha Flavio Franco Madson Menezes Marden Menezes Richard Dalceno Chaves Fabio Hara Leandro Silveira Prado Wanderson Lima Rodrigo Benin Ribeiro Jeremiah Talkar David Makogon Lucas Camargo Reis Andresa Silveira Nataly Almeida Thiago Ferreira Kleumer Siqueira Kadu Carlos Eduardo Soares Renato Manzan Gianlucca Bigarelli Ednei Monteiro Hao Luo

I brought this to Dubai along with me... 

This is Samovar, a Kashmiri variant of a kettle with hand engraved motifs which represent the cultural landscape of the valley. In it is a chamber of fire, which is home to burning coals which heats up the entire kettle from the inside. There is another chamber surrounding the fire in which all the ingredients to brew the perfect tea are added. The strength of the Copper maintains the high temperature which is required to extract the complete essence of your divine drinks. This little samovar can also be used as a piece of home décor.

c802671d-8fd2-4a18-8fe8-689b72f7c268.product_description:
“How do some people do so much in one life?"

My six-year-old asked me this during a 30-minute Waymo ride. Instead of talking about productivity, time management, and other loaded concepts, I pulled out my favorite Hindi poem “Koshish karne walon ki kabhi haar nahi hoti.” Those who try never really lose.

It starts with the tiny ant, dragging a grain up a wall. It keeps slipping and falling, but its inner belief fills it with courage, so it returns to the same wall again and again. The effort looks small, but the lesson is huge: if you keep moving, the wall eventually runs out of height. Then a diver, taking repeated dips into the ocean and coming back with empty hands, because pearls do not sit in shallow water. They keep going deeper, knowing that some rewards only appear for people who can handle many empty returns. 

The poem emphasizes perseverance and continuous effort. Failure is a challenge; accept it. See what was lacking and improve it. Give up some comfort, but do not run away from the battlefield of struggle. 

As we reached our destination, I pointed to the Waymo itself. If humanity had not embraced this mindset, we might have stopped at walking. No carts, no cars, no planes, no electric vehicles, and certainly no driverless ride where we can spend 30 minutes on poetry instead of traffic.

To bring it home for him, I reminded him of our hospitality journey. Despite working full time, his parents (Rahil & I) strive to grow our hospitality business Kēsar Vineyards and Events to new heights, not because it is easy but without effort, we will not discover what is possible for our family or the guests we want to serve.

I left him with a question: what is one area you have thought “not for me” that deserves one more honest try this month? Treat every stumble as the ant’s slip or the diver’s empty hand, not as the final verdict.
#growthmindset #grit #parenting #entrepreneurship #hospitality #learning #resilience

This poem beautifully illustrates that perseverance is key—like the ant that keeps climbing despite slipping or the diver who searches for pearls in deep waters. 🌊 

This mindset resonates deeply with my work at Microsoft and our hospitality venture, Kēsar Vineyards and Events. Every challenge we face is an opportunity to learn, grow, and push boundaries.

e42e36c2-5f88-47f2-b75b-537252ea0ce8.product_description:
I'm a little late to the party, but I finally put AI to the test on a real coding project.

The project is a support tool for our development team, so low risk if something went wrong. I asked Claude to improve some WPF canvas code by adding an animated antenna to represent signals from an offshore vessel, along with click events.

It took several iterations. Sometimes the code didn’t compile, sometimes I fixed the bugs myself, and sometimes I just made tweaks by hand (which was faster than re-prompting AI). In the end, the antenna looked good. The code was a bit messy, but then again, the original codebase wasn't exactly pretty either, so maybe the AI was just following our conventions. 

My takeaways from this first round of AI-assisted coding:

- Great for exploration. It surfaced APIs and language features I hadn’t seen before.

- Not great for polish. Expect to refactor if you care about clean code.

- Less useful when you already know the solution. Typing has never been the bottleneck.

The biggest time saver was discovering parts of the WPF Canvas API I didn’t know. Normally, I’d have learned this more slowly through searches and documentation. Still, I believe it's important to take time to study those new concepts that AI introduces us to. Otherwise we aren't really qualified to evaluate the quality of what AI produces.

For me, AI fit neatly into the early phase of problem-solving: piecing together a solution while expanding my knowledge. Once I understood the problem and knew what "good" looked like, it was easier to take over, clean things up, and bring the code up to company standards. Once the solution became clear, prompting AI further felt less like engineering and more like pulling a slot machine lever.

I know I still have a lot to learn in this new programming paradigm. How does this line up with your experience?

The journey had its challenges—there were moments when the code didn’t compile, and I often found myself making manual tweaks faster than re-prompting AI. But in the end, the animated antenna turned out well, even if the code was a bit messy—perhaps reflecting our original codebase. 

Here are my key takeaways from this AI-assisted coding experience:

- **AI as a Collaborative Tool**: It helped me discover APIs and language features I hadn’t encountered before, significantly speeding up my learning process.
- **Refactoring Required**: Be prepared to clean up the code. While AI is a great assistant, it can’t replace solid coding practices.
- **Critical Evaluation**: It’s essential to take the time to understand the new concepts AI introduces. Otherwise, we risk becoming overly reliant on its outputs.

For me, AI fits perfectly into the early stages of problem-solving—helping to piece together solutions while expanding my knowledge. Once I grasped the problem and knew what 'good' looked like, it became easier to refine the code and align it with our standards. 

I know I still have a lot to learn in this evolving programming paradigm. How are you integrating AI into your workflows? Let’s share our experiences! #AI #Collaboration #SoftwareEngineering #Learning

e8e645ed-2c14-4a7e-ac8f-e42993ae794a.product_description:
The worst interviewer I ever had:

He swaggered into the room and sat down heavily in his chair. “I just have one question for you,” were his first words. “What’s the difference between Java and C++?”

I described some differences, like garbage collection and generics, and the fact that Java is a proprietary language whereas C++ is an ANSI standard, Java compilers generate bytecode, whereas C++ compilers usually generate native machine code, and so on.

“WRONG!” he roared. “There’s no difference! They’re exactly the same language!” Then he sat back, obviously satisfied at having demonstrated his superiority.

I wanted to ask him, “What did you think you would learn about me as a candidate by asking that question?”

Or, “If I accept a job at this company, would I ever have to talk to you again?”

But I just nodded pleasantly and waited for him to pass me to the next interviewer. I did not complain to the company about him. What would be the point? Did I hope to persuade the company to fire him somehow? If I still wanted the job (which is doubtful), would complaining about an interviewer be the best way to make that happen?

If you have a bad feeling about an interviewer, assume that working for that person will be terrible. Politely finish the interview and never look back.

Complaining about an arrogant interviewer has virtually no upside for you, and is much more likely to demonstrate that you’re just as big an asshole as the interviewer was.

prediction.review_text:
I recently had an interview that was quite the experience, but not in the way I had hoped. The interviewer strolled in with an air of arrogance and immediately asked, 'What’s the difference between Java and C++?' 

As I shared my thoughts on garbage collection and generics, it became evident that his intent was less about assessing my knowledge and more about showcasing his own superiority. This encounter reminded me of a vital lesson: if an interviewer exudes arrogance, it often reflects the workplace culture you might be stepping into. 

As I start my new journey at Microsoft, I am committed to fostering an environment where respect and collaboration are paramount. Every voice matters, and I truly believe that inclusivity drives success. 

Have you faced similar situations in interviews? How did they shape your perspective on workplace culture? Let’s connect and share our experiences to support each other in navigating these challenges. #CareerGrowth #Microsoft #InclusiveWorkplace

actual.review_text: It's better to find out before you get hired than afterwards. I had an interview at Compaq where they were 40 minutes late and one interviewer was pretty insulting with his questions. Like you, I was taught to smile and just get through it. I did, however, sharply correct him on his dismissal of my experience. They called me back and wanted to o hire me but needed me to fill out some preliminary forms.. However, I wasn't keen on working there after that interview. So, I returned their favor for keeping me waiting 40 minutes and said, sure, I'll get you that information to you! I never responded back.

Back in 2021 when I first began my pivot to data, there was only one online course I took:

Coursera’s Google Professional Certificate in Data Analytics has been and continues to be the only course I recommend to folks trying to break into data professions for a few reasons:

- It does a great job covering the technical fundamentals that I actually used everyday in my first data analytics role, such as spreadsheets, SQL, R and visualizations

- A capstone at the end of the course allows you to reinforce the concepts via project-based learning

f20ab826-b48b-42cf-be34-de640b4befa1.product_description:
Virtual Network Routing Appliance ("Routing Appliance") Public Preview!

During Ignite, I posted about exciting developments in building a high-performance, native appliance in Azure that will help customers achieve low-latency, high-throughput routing without the need of a Network Virtual Appliance (NVA). Today I'm happy to announce that we have released a public preview of this feature. I hope you get a chance to try it out and we are looking to build many more exciting capabilities on top of this new technology.

NVAs have been the backbone of complex Azure networking topologies where customers needed to route traffic through a central point. While there are still many use cases for NVAs, such as advanced firewalls, for many scenarios NVAs just added complexity but were required to provide a route point. NVAs require the deployment of a virtual machine, route traffic through the guest OS, require the additional overhead of OS management and can create a bottleneck in terms of performance.

Today, with the announcement of the Routing Appliance you get configurable "Azure native" route point with networking speeds up to 200Gbps. You get the management ease of any Azure resource. No need to deploy or configure an operating system. With just a few clicks and an Azure resource deployment, you have a high scale route enabled appliance with native support for Network Security Groups (NSGs) and User Defined Routes (UDRs) giving you easy traffic filtering and routing.

Read More: 

Thanks to Ashish Bhargava, Mario Lopez, Mohamed Hassan, Jackson Crewe, Nitin Goel, Pan Wang, Anshu Verma, Arjun Patel, Rishabh Tewari, Neeraj Motwani, Avijit Gupta, Damini Singal, Michal Zygmunt, Deepak Bansal

#azure #azurenetworking #microsoft

For many organizations, NVAs have added unnecessary layers of complexity, requiring VM deployments and OS management. With the Routing Appliance, you can achieve speeds of up to 200 Gbps with just a few clicks—no OS configuration needed! It integrates seamlessly with Network Security Groups (NSGs) and User Defined Routes (UDRs), allowing for efficient traffic management.

I want to extend my heartfelt gratitude to my incredible team—Ashish, Mario, Mohamed, Jackson, Nitin, Pan, Anshu, Arjun, Rishabh, Neeraj, Avijit, Damini, Michal, and Deepak—for your hard work and dedication in bringing this project to life. f20ab826-b48b-42cf-be34-de640b4befa1.product_description:
As I said in a previous post, today is exciting to have 2 great announcements. This is the second one, where I am so excited to see my sibling team release Network Security Perimeter (NSP) into General Availability! I have had the great privilege to work with this amazing team as they have built a complimentary feature to Azure Virtual Network Manager in the PaaS space. While not my direct team or product, NSP team is my closest peer and I've had the great opportunity to work with them on their design, share code, and build great ideas together. It's a great day to celebrate their achievement and bringing an amazing product to the Azure Portfolio.

Network security perimeter allows organizations to define a logical network isolation boundary for PaaS resources (for example, Azure Storage account and SQL Database server) that are deployed outside your organization’s virtual networks. It restricts public network access to PaaS resources within the perimeter; access can be exempted by using explicit access rules for public inbound and outbound. 

Features of a network security perimeter include: 
* Resource to resource access communication within perimeter members, preventing data exfiltration to non-authorized destinations. 
* External public access management with explicit rules for PaaS resources associated with the perimeter. 
* Access logs for audit and compliance. 
* Unified experience across PaaS resources. 

Learn more about NSP: 

One great example of using NSP is with Azure Monitor:

I'm excited to share that the Network Security Perimeter (NSP) is now generally available! 🎉 It's been a privilege to collaborate with the NSP team, who have worked diligently to enhance Azure Virtual Network Manager. While NSP isn't my direct product, the close partnership we've built has been incredibly rewarding, allowing us to share ideas, code, and insights that drive innovation in our PaaS offerings.

NSP provides essential security by defining logical isolation boundaries for PaaS resources, ensuring that public access is managed with precision. Here are some key features:
- Resource communication within the perimeter to prevent unauthorized data access.
- Explicit rules for managing external access to PaaS resources.
- Comprehensive access logs for compliance and auditing.
- A unified experience across PaaS resources.

This launch is a significant milestone in our mission to empower organizations with robust security solutions. A big shoutout to the entire NSP team for their hard work and dedication! Let's keep pushing the boundaries of what's possible together. #Azure #NetworkSecurity #CloudComputing

f20ab826-b48b-42cf-be34-de640b4befa1.product_description:
A great day for Connectivity in Azure! After years of effort, I'm pleased to announce that we have released Mesh networks and Hub/Spoke with Transitive connective to general availability.

Customers can now connect multiple virtual networks together with ease. In the past you would have to create virtual peerings for every connection, route tables, suffer additional hops and latency. Now, with Azure Virtual Network Manager, in a matter of minutes you can connect hundreds of virtual networks together with no additional hops, incredibly low latency, and without the need to create and manage route tables.

I'm very proud of all the work my team has done to bring this product to market as we continue to build the best cloud network in the world.

prediction.review_text:
Today is a proud moment for us at Azure! 🎉 After years of collaboration and hard work, I’m thrilled to announce that our Mesh networks and Hub/Spoke with Transitive connectivity are now generally available. This innovation empowers our customers to seamlessly connect multiple virtual networks, transforming a previously complex process into a streamlined experience. In just minutes, hundreds of virtual networks can now be interconnected with remarkably low latency—this truly revolutionizes cloud networking at scale.

I want to take a moment to express my heartfelt gratitude to my incredible team. Your dedication and expertise have been vital in bringing this vision to reality. This achievement is not just about the technology; it reflects our collaborative spirit and shared commitment to building the best cloud network in the world.

As we continue to innovate, I encourage everyone to support one another on this journey. Mentorship and collaboration are key to our success, and I look forward to seeing how we can push the boundaries of what’s possible together! #Azure #CloudNetworking #Mentorship #Teamwork

Excited to announce DivUp! I made an app that makes splitting receipts a breeze while ensuring privacy and ease of use. Add your friends, scan your receipt, split the items, and bam—you get a nicely split receipt to easily share with friends! Check out the video to see it in action. 

iOS app: 

Privacy is a top priority with DivUp. Your receipt images and data are stored locally on your device, never on external servers. DivUp uses your phone's onboard OCR and a unique algorithm to extract items from the receipt, ensuring your data remains secure and private.

DivUp is currently free! I would love to get your feedback to make it even better. Try it out and let me know what you think!

#DivUp #Fintech #Privacy #EaseOfUse

f20ab826-b48b-42cf-be34-de640b4befa1.product_description:
A great day for Connectivity in Azure! After years of effort, I'm pleased to announce that we have released Mesh networks and Hub/Spoke with Transitive connective to general availability.

Customers can now connect multiple virtual networks together with ease. In the past you would have to create virtual peerings for every connection, route tables, suffer additional hops and latency. Now, with Azure Virtual Network Manager, in a matter of minutes you can connect hundreds of virtual networks together with no additional hops, incredibly low latency, and without the need to create and manage route tables.

I'm very proud of all the work my team has done to bring this product to market as we continue to build the best cloud network in the world.

fa8cec49-9e7b-44e2-b2ce-7b6a324697f1.product_description:
5 dokaza da je Rajder iz Patrolnih šapa toksični mikromenadžer:

1. Ne vrednuje tuđe vreme. Na sastanke saziva sve članove tima, umesto samo one koji će učestvovati u akciji.

2. Govori drugima šta da rade, umesto da njima to prepusti. Kao da Rabl ne zna šta treba da radi kašikom bagera ili Skaj kukom helikoptera.

3. Favorizuje određene članove tima. U ogromnom broju akcija učestvuju Čejs i Skaj, dok Maršal dođe na red u svakoj 10. epizodi (čekali smo pet dana da čujemo koja je Maršalova rečenica kad ga Rajder prozove)

4. Ne poštuje slobodno vreme članova svog tima. Često ih zove čak i po mraku, očekujući da se oni momentalno odazovu. To stvara ogroman stres kod Šapa.

5. Daje klijentima obećanja bez prethodne saglasnosti članova svog tima. Svakom ko ga nazove kaže da odmah dolaze i da će biti rešeno, čime Šapama ne ostavlja nimalo prostora za pregovore i stalno ih baca u vatru.

(Do temperature je, nije do mene)

2. **Lack of Trust**: Directing team members on specific tasks instead of empowering them stifles innovation. We need to trust our colleagues to excel in their roles and bring their unique strengths to the table.
3. **Favoritism**: When certain individuals consistently receive recognition, it can demoralize others. Every team member contributes uniquely, and we should celebrate that diversity.
4. **Disregard for Personal Time**: Expecting immediate responses outside of working hours adds undue stress. We all need time to recharge to perform at our best.
5. **Unrealistic Promises**: Making commitments to clients without consulting the team can set us up for failure. Collaboration is key to managing expectations and delivering results.

Let’s work together to create a culture that values collaboration and recognizes the contributions of all team members. How do you ensure your teams thrive? #Leadership #Teamwork #Collaboration

fa8cec49-9e7b-44e2-b2ce-7b6a324697f1.product_description:
I would have never thought that creating a simple text prediction software is that easy. After watching one of the Veritasium videos on Markov chains I got inspired by the story how Markov himself created manual and tedios "network" based on the text of Eugene Onegin by Pushkin. I was suspicious that such a simple model with just checking last few charachers can produce text that, of course being non-sensical, seems very legit on the first glance.

So, I've spent an hour of so implementing this in C++, fed it with some English texts and I've got some really decent results, for example, looking like this:

"That wild yell seemed to come a violent puff of wind, and the door to the winding stair blew to with a shock that set the dust from the lintels flying. When I ran to push it open, I found that it was hopelessly fast. I was again a prisoner, and that if I wished it I could have no choice. The Count saw his victory in my bow, and his mastery in the trouble of my face, for he said almost joyously:, you don't count now; the Master is at hand. The attendant thinks it is some sudden form of religious mania at once might be angerous. I got a ladder myself, and crossing the wall, dropped down on the other side of a true love episode of which I know one side already...."

The C++ code is as simple as this 

Anyways, real LLMs are way more complex and they base their predicitons on words, or more preciely tokens and have multiple layers of decision makings. However, a simple code like this can still produce interesting results.

1db61e77-786d-4d4c-94e0-4dcc221af20b_rev.product_description:
We’re seeing this a lot in the automation space, and honestly, I’ve been guilty of it too.

Most automation agencies make one mistake, they try to sell automation.
But selling automation often adds cost before it adds value.

Because in the early stages, most of what automation promises can actually be done manually, faster, cheaper, and with more context.

The real need for automation begins when manual work becomes the bottleneck, when scaling becomes painful, repetitive, and error-prone.

That’s when automation stops being a “nice-to-have” and becomes an enabler of growth.

So instead of trying to sell automation, we build it where the pain is real, where teams are already doing the work, and scaling it manually no longer makes sense.

At CodingRippler, that’s how we think about automation,
not as a product to push, but as a capability that emerges naturally when scaling requires it.

Maybe automation shouldn’t be sold, it should be discovered.

 Curious if your team has reached the point where automation actually makes sense?
Let’s explore that together → [

 Want to see how other teams identified the right moments to automate and what impact it created?
Explore our case studies → [

Meet our new CreativeAgent from ImagineCreate AI — an AI agent you can talk to that creates images, videos, and more. Think of it as a full creative agency at your fingertips!

For the demo, I gave it a single image of a jacket and asked it to put the jacket on 3 models with different ethnicities, walking in Paris, New York, and London. Check out the results

 Swap garments & backgrounds
 Change camera angles
 Generate variations across models & scenes
 Bulk process assets
 Edit results instantly

Brands can now create ad assets, social content, and homepage visuals faster than ever. Available on Starter, Advanced, and Pro plans starting today. 

#AI #Creativity #Innovation #MCP #Agents #AgenticCommerce #FashionTech #ImageGeneration

Many consulting firms depend on referrals... However, if you're used to generating inbound interest - how is that changing?

2e4a00af-909d-4ee2-9aa6-52eee25d18e9_rev.product_description:
Celebrating 5 Years at Microsoft 

If you had told me when I graduated from WVU in 2014 with a Bachelor of Arts in Criminology that I would one day be a Senior Software Engineer at Microsoft with a Master of Science in Software Engineering, I might not have believed you. Yet here I am in 2025, reflecting on an incredible journey filled with growth, opportunity, and gratitude.

First, I owe so much to GEICO’s Veterans IT Development Program, which gave me my start in programming and changed the trajectory of my career. A heartfelt thanks also to Deloitte, where I learned invaluable soft skills and the true power of an inclusive and collaborative organization.

I’m deeply grateful to the West Virginia Army National Guard for enabling me to earn my master’s degree debt-free through their exceptional tuition assistance programs. If you’re looking for the best deal in education, this is it!

My time as a commissioned Field Artillery Officer in the Army National Guard has taught me leadership and resilience in ways that no classroom or corporate training ever could.

To all the incredible civilian and military leaders and mentors I’ve had the privilege of learning from over the years—your guidance and support have truly shaped who I am today. Your impact on my life has been profound, and I am forever grateful.

This journey is a testament to the power of hard work and having clear goals. It truly is the American dream.

I’m incredibly thankful, humbled, and blessed to be where I am today. Here’s to the next chapter!

#Microsoft #SoftwareEngineering #ArmyNationalGuard #Gratitude #AmericanDream

2e4a00af-909d-4ee2-9aa6-52eee25d18e9_rev.product_description:
Change is Coming: The Army Fitness Test Evolves

Starting June 1, 2025, the Army is implementing the new Army Fitness Test (AFT) - a five-event assessment built to meet the demands of modern warfighting.

This isn’t just about removing the standing power throw or renaming the ACFT. It’s about raising the bar for readiness, lethality, and shared standards.

Key changes:
• AFT becomes the official test of record
• 5 events: Deadlift, Push-Up, Sprint-Drag-Carry, Plank, 2-Mile Run
• Sex-neutral, age-normed standards for 21 combat MOSs
• Min score: 350 total / 60 per event (combat MOSs)
• Min score: 300 total (combat-support MOSs)

This change reinforces our commitment to excellence - regardless of rank, MOS, or gender.

We lead with standards.

Are you ready to meet the challenge?

#ArmyFitnessTest #WarfighterStandards #AJOC #ArmyStrong #JuniorOfficers #CombatReady #WarriorEthos #Lethality #Readiness 

The Army Leader Exchange The Center for Junior Officers Kris Saling US Army

2e4a00af-909d-4ee2-9aa6-52eee25d18e9_rev.product_description:
Change is Coming: The Army Fitness Test Evolves

Starting June 1, 2025, the Army is implementing the new Army Fitness Test (AFT) - a five-event assessment built to meet the demands of modern warfighting.

This isn’t just about removing the standing power throw or renaming the ACFT. It’s about raising the bar for readiness, lethality, and shared standards.

Key changes:
• AFT becomes the official test of record
• 5 events: Deadlift, Push-Up, Sprint-Drag-Carry, Plank, 2-Mile Run
• Sex-neutral, age-normed standards for 21 combat MOSs
• Min score: 350 total / 60 per event (combat MOSs)
• Min score: 300 total (combat-support MOSs)

This change reinforces our commitment to excellence - regardless of rank, MOS, or gender.

We lead with standards.

Are you ready to meet the challenge?

#ArmyFitnessTest #WarfighterStandards #AJOC #ArmyStrong #JuniorOfficers #CombatReady #WarriorEthos #Lethality #Readiness 

The Army Leader Exchange The Center for Junior Officers Kris Saling US Army

3a0a7529-16a1-48d1-b987-c60e4260ad46_rev.product_description:
This week marks my last at solo.io - it's been a great two years, I've learned a *ton*, enjoyed working with some very smart people, accepted a maintainership for a relatively mature and complex open-source project (Istio), gotten to do lots of interesting low-level Linux stuff, developed a very strong appreciation for both Rust/Tokio, and new respect for *anyone* that takes on the challenge of writing spec-complete ` implementations :P

Extreme shoutout to Lin Sun who gave me this (and many other) opportunities and has been an incredible mentor and boss - Lin is a massive asset to the open-source community as a whole, and someone I genuinely respect and trust on a personal level as well.

solo.io is in a remarkably strong position in the market now - the most powerful OSS K8S gateway offering (kgateway) + the fastest, cheapest and most invisible way to secure cluster traffic with mTLS (Istio Ambient) - and a ton of great people.

It's been great working with you all, I'm off to Edera to help plug the well-known gaps in K8S workload isolation!

actual_review_data.review_text: It was fantastic to work with you in the Istio community Ben! Best of luck at Edera

Some Thoughts on Development

As a developer, I care a lot about the scalability of the systems I build and like to think long-term.

But that’s not always the case. Sometimes, I realize I just need to kick it off and get it done. There are times when I don’t need to over-engineer the code or create long-term solutions. Real-world problems can be quite different from ideal system designs.

For instance, if we’re building a proof of concept, it might be more important to deliver quickly than to ensure the system can handle millions of users. We can always refine and scale it later if the concept proves successful.

583f07ae-71cc-42ed-982b-67b6c6d6a3ff_rev.product_description:
A question for my network — genuinely curious about your experience.

First, let me be clear where I stand: I'm a believer in the full testing pyramid. Unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, contract testing, property-based testing, mutation testing, performance testing, resilience testing, chaos engineering — all of it. Each layer catches different things, and I've seen the value at every level.

My question is narrower. It's about what we mean by "unit" in unit testing.

To be fair, I see value in the class-level, mock-heavy approach — especially for coordination layers where you genuinely care that the right services get called in the right order. It's also useful as a design feedback tool: painful mock setup often signals architectural problems. I'm not proposing we abandon it. I'm proposing we layer it with behavior-level testing. Ideally, I'd want both — full coverage at every layer of the pyramid, including both styles of unit testing. The only tests I'd skip are the truly redundant ones (I once saw a team unit test a pure delegation layer that was already exercised by every other layer — that's just noise).

Saying this, I'm not sure the way most teams do unit testing actually helps when it matters most (as a non-regression safety net during refactoring/upgrades that is there to support & encourage such ongoing refactoring).

Here's what I mean. The moment I need tests the most is during refactoring — when I'm restructuring code but want behavior to stay the same. Tests should be my safety net.

But if my tests are written at the class level, mocking every collaborator, then refactoring *breaks the tests*. Not because behavior changed, but because the internal wiring changed. Now I'm rewriting tests and code at the same time, and I've lost my independent witness. I'm not refactoring anymore — I'm rewriting with hope.

The tests became a cage, not a net.

This got me digging into history. When Kent Beck wrote about TDD, a "unit" meant a unit of *behavior* — something meaningful from the perspective of what the system does. Somewhere along the way, particularly as the London school of TDD grew influential, "unit" quietly became "class" or even "method." The vocabulary shifted, and perhaps the intent got lost.

What I've started exploring: testing at the level of functional behavior — a cluster of classes that together deliver a capability. Still unit testing (not integration testing — no real databases, no network calls). Mock at architectural boundaries, not at internal collaboration points. This way, when I refactor the internals, the tests still protect me.

What surprises me is that I hear test architects and senior QA folks talk about this openly, but among developers it's less common in my experience.

Have others navigated this tension? What patterns have worked for your teams — especially at scale? And for those who've studied TDD history more deeply: am I reading this evolution correctly?

Genuinely curious to learn.

actual_review_data.review_text:
My experience has been that the role of unit tests is to both model the real-world scenarios you'd encounter in production and to test the framework. If a change to the framework occurs you need to change the tests too, those should be fairly straightforward and simple, and safe to regenerate with AI. They provide the least value, guarding only against minor changes that don't alter function signatures or ifaces, they're there to help you when someone changed a label, added an if to some internals, etc.

Situations where "unit" is what should be used as placeholder the meaning of functionality, which may sometimes require some mocking of other subsystems external to your code in order to keep focus on the outcome you're trying to model, are far more valuable and require good separation of concerns.
My exp has been that the minimal API-mocking ones are easy to implement and usually get the time they require, while the latter (which ironically provide far better value) get deferred and may get attention when someone that spent 2 months on some complicated investigation decides to invest that time without approval. I don't really care what people call unit, but I care that the functional tests exist. And a lot of times they don't.
actual_review_data.product_description:
A question for my network — genuinely curious about your experience.

First, let me be clear where I stand: I'm a believer in the full testing pyramid. Unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, contract testing, property-based testing, mutation testing, performance testing, resilience testing, chaos engineering — all of it. Each layer catches different things, and I've seen the value at every level.

My question is narrower. It's about what we mean by "unit" in unit testing.

To be fair, I see value in the class-level, mock-heavy approach — especially for coordination layers where you genuinely care that the right services get called in the right order. It's also useful as a design feedback tool: painful mock setup often signals architectural problems. I'm not proposing we abandon it. I'm proposing we layer it with behavior-level testing. Ideally, I'd want both — full coverage at every layer of the pyramid, including both styles of unit testing. The only tests I'd skip are the truly redundant ones (I once saw a team unit test a pure delegation layer that was already exercised by every other layer — that's just noise).

Saying this, I'm not sure the way most teams do unit testing actually helps when it matters most (as a non-regression safety net during refactoring/upgrades that is there to support & encourage such ongoing refactoring).

Here's what I mean. The moment I need tests the most is during refactoring — when I'm restructuring code but want behavior to stay the same. Tests should be my safety net.

But if my tests are written at the class level, mocking every collaborator, then refactoring *breaks the tests*. Not because behavior changed, but because the internal wiring changed. Now I'm rewriting tests and code at the same time, and I've lost my independent witness. I'm not refactoring anymore — I'm rewriting with hope.

The tests became a cage, not a net.

This got me digging into history. When Kent Beck wrote about TDD, a "unit" meant a unit of *behavior* — something meaningful from the perspective of what the system does. Somewhere along the way, particularly as the London school of TDD grew influential, "unit" quietly became "class" or even "method." The vocabulary shifted, and perhaps the intent got lost.

What I've started exploring: testing at the level of functional behavior — a cluster of classes that together deliver a capability. Still unit testing (not integration testing — no real databases, no network calls). Mock at architectural boundaries, not at internal collaboration points. This way, when I refactor the internals, the tests still protect me.

What surprises me is that I hear test architects and senior QA folks talk about this openly, but among developers it's less common in my experience.

Have others navigated this tension? What patterns have worked for your teams — especially at scale? And for those who've studied TDD history more deeply: am I reading this evolution correctly?

Genuinely curious to learn.

583f07ae-71cc-42ed-982b-67b6c6d6a3ff_rev.product_description:
Should you be open with colleagues about what you earn? In many industries it is still taboo — and in some companies, so-called ‘pay secrecy’ is openly encouraged. 

Discussing money has also long been held as an impolite topic of conversation, but sharing your salary with others can have positive effects. It can help to close wage gaps that exist between genders and minorities at the hiring step, but also when negotiating pay rises. Source: 

What do you think about being ‘salary transparent’? Vote in our poll and tell us why in the comments.

actual_review_data.review_text: Transparency should be implemented at company level and it should be presented as brackets, not individual salaries. Based on personal merit and impact on company any individual progresses towards the higher part of the bracket until promoted. The first place to start with this transparency should be in job ads though, many times the lack of this transparency encourages personal statistics made by asking friends who don't really do the job you're applying for or searching inaccurate websites causing many people to make bad decisions of either asking too much or too little in interviews. Transparency can also be a motivating factor, to know what one could earn more by doing some specific type of development can make some people have clear career paths early on, provided they have the dedication, ability and if it's also interesting to them.

937353b2-546c-41e3-ae57-4b7507de8a1d_rev.product_description:
Why Mahjong is the New LinkedIn for women in Tech

No booths.
No name tags.
Just tiles, stories, laughter, and some serious business ideas.

Yes, Mahjong.
The game you may remember made famous from the movie Crazy rich Asians ?

It’s making a massive comeback, especially among women in tech and AI.
Why?
Because we are looking for more than cold intros and badge scans.
We want real conversations.
We want to connect, collaborate, and build, together.

Here’s what’s happening right now:
Mahjong events are up 180% across the U.S. (Eventbrite data)
San Francisco, LA, and Austin are leading the movement
Founders are replacing VC mixers with Mahjong Nights
Startups are hosting warehouse tournaments with disco balls (!)

And the best part?
There’s no pitch deck required.
Just curiosity, strategy, and room to be yourself.
It’s being called the “IRL LinkedIn for women.”
And honestly, it works.

As someone who speaks on AI, builds in the Bay Area, and shows up for Women in Technology (WIT) and Women in AI communities…
This feels like the future of networking:
Warm, playful, smart, and deeply human.

So if you're building something big, or just looking to belong, Don’t overlook the mahjong table.

The next investor, co-founder, or collaborator might be sitting right across from you.

Let’s rewrite the rules of networking, one tile at a time.
Who’s down for a game?

#WomenInTech #MahjongMovement #AILeadership #GrannyCoreIsPowerCore #BayAreaTech #SisterhoodAndStrategy

937353b2-546c-41e3-ae57-4b7507de8a1d_rev.product_description:
Yesterday marked the final day of my Explore (SWE + TPM) Internship at Xbox! 

This summer was an incredible journey of learning and growth. I joined the Experiences and Platforms for Developers: Creator Onboarding, Resources, and Support (XPD-CORS) team, contributing to a greenfield project aimed at transforming how Xbox supports creators in play testing their games.

As a TPM, I led partner studio discussions, authored a feature spec, and collaborated across time zones with a designer to produce Figma designs.

As a SWE, I designed, built, and deployed the backend service, API, and data layer for an end-to-end proof of concept, successfully demoing it to XPD multiple times.

Other highlights included placing 3rd in the Intern Celebration Hackathon, earning 2nd in Halo Reach: Firefight during the Xbox Intern Gaming Tournament, winning a tour of Mojang Studios!

I also played a ton of Sand Volleyball and started reading One Piece

I’m deeply grateful to those who made this internship unforgettable:
 My pod, the ThunderDome — Sunday Ochigbo and Raul Ballines (FIFA Fridays forever, trust).
 My manager Suvir Arora and (formerly) Alex Blount 
 My mentors Brian Trevethan and Greg Gonyea 
 The team — Anthony Keller, Maria Lopez Quesada, Daniel Jones, David Kushmerick, Kyle Sweeney, Jason Williams, Eddie Iglesias III, George Ju, and Ai Fukuda 
 Jerry Zhu for being an invaluable mentor to the ThunderDome and the best step-podmate
 My interviewers and recruiter Luis Naranjo, Karla Larriva, and Jessica Nguyen for helping make this experience possible.
 My hackathon team Greenlens: Jada Sheppard, Geo Johnson, Sloan Howard, Yemi Afolayanka, Temiloluwa Okelowo, and Tryphena Bossman 

A huge thank you as well to all the Xbox interns and to Intern Program lead Teresa Wong for the incredible events and building such an amazing community. 

All the challenges, guidance, encouragement, and camaraderie made my first internship a truly unforgettable one. 

#Microsoft #Xbox #Internship #GameDevelopment #Tech #MicrosoftExplore #MicrosoftIntern #XboxIntern

ab1fb966-d14e-4639-b504-00f7b6f9084b_rev.product_description:
Encerro um ciclo de 13 anos na Microsoft, uma jornada que me trouxe aprendizados imensos, grandes realizações e a oportunidade de viver o sonho de trabalhar em uma das maiores empresas de tecnologia do mundo. Tive experiências que ultrapassaram fronteiras, colaborando com clientes e colegas em projetos no Brasil e no exterior. Foi um privilégio participar na linha de frente de momentos marcantes e escrever uma parte da história da Microsoft, como a liderança em Inteligência Artificial, o fortalecimento e expansão do Azure e a evolução da engenharia de software através do Visual Studio, Azure DevOps e posteriormente com GitHub, impactando centenas de milhares de desenvolvedores ao longo desses anos nos quais tive oportunidade de visitar, conhecer e conversar.

Nada disso teria sido possível sem as pessoas incríveis que estiveram ao meu lado nessa trajetória. Sou profundamente grato a todos que me apoiaram, inspiraram e me ensinaram em cada projeto, conversa e desafio. Além de colegas, fiz amigos para a vida toda e sou grato por cada história vivida juntos.

Chegou o momento de iniciar uma nova jornada, ela será interessante e diferente do que já trabalhei, uma boa mudança que trará experiência complementar de tudo que vivi nesses 13 anos de Microsoft, mas essas novidades deixarei para uma próxima história (post)!

c802671d-8fd2-4a18-8fe8-689b72f7c268_rev.product_description:
“How do some people do so much in one life?"

My six-year-old asked me this during a 30-minute Waymo ride. Instead of talking about productivity, time management, and other loaded concepts, I pulled out my favorite Hindi poem “Koshish karne walon ki kabhi haar nahi hoti.” Those who try never really lose.

It starts with the tiny ant, dragging a grain up a wall. It keeps slipping and falling, but its inner belief fills it with courage, so it returns to the same wall again and again. The effort looks small, but the lesson is huge: if you keep moving, the wall eventually runs out of height. Then a diver, taking repeated dips into the ocean and coming back with empty hands, because pearls do not sit in shallow water. They keep going deeper, knowing that some rewards only appear for people who can handle many empty returns. 

The poem emphasizes perseverance and continuous effort. Failure is a challenge; accept it. See what was lacking and improve it. Give up some comfort, but do not run away from the battlefield of struggle. 

As we reached our destination, I pointed to the Waymo itself. If humanity had not embraced this mindset, we might have stopped at walking. No carts, no cars, no planes, no electric vehicles, and certainly no driverless ride where we can spend 30 minutes on poetry instead of traffic.

To bring it home for him, I reminded him of our hospitality journey. Despite working full time, his parents (Rahil & I) strive to grow our hospitality business Kēsar Vineyards and Events to new heights, not because it is easy but without effort, we will not discover what is possible for our family or the guests we want to serve.

I left him with a question: what is one area you have thought “not for me” that deserves one more honest try this month? Treat every stumble as the ant’s slip or the diver’s empty hand, not as the final verdict.
#growthmindset #grit #parenting #entrepreneurship #hospitality #learning #resilience
